---
title: "Evaluation Planning and Design"
description: "Study note on Evaluation Planning and Design in Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation."
book_id: "handbook-practical-program-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Major section"
source_pages: "43-45"
citation_key: "newcomer_hatry_wholey_2015_handbook_practical_program_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-1:c0001"
  - "chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-1:c0004"
source_status: "needs_review"
---
## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2015</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Source pages 43-45</span><span>Major section</span></div>
This note summarises the MCP research-library section "Evaluation Planning and Design" from Kathryn E. Newcomer, Harry P. Hatry, and Joseph S. Wholey's *Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation* [@newcomer_hatry_wholey_2015_handbook_practical_program_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked retrieval records, not a substitute for the source text.

## Core Argument

The part overview frames planning and design as the work of producing credible, useful evaluation evidence under real constraints.

## Study Summary

The part overview frames planning and design as the work of producing credible, useful evaluation evidence under real constraints.

It covers stakeholder engagement, logic modeling, evaluability assessment, monitoring, impact designs, case studies, multisite work, and culturally responsive evaluation.

The recurring design tension is between the precision and generalizability desired and the time and resources available.

## Key Concepts

- Design trade-offs
- Stakeholder engagement
- Evaluation approach
- Usefulness

## Source Location

- Section path: Part 1: Evaluation Planning and Design
- Evidence records: `chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-1:c0001`, `chunk:handbook-practical-program-evaluation:part-1:c0004`

## Practical Implications

- Plan evaluation around information needs, not only available methods.
- Make cost, rigor, and timing trade-offs explicit.
- Choose design options that stakeholders can act on.

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Concepts: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Map: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What is the main evaluation problem that Evaluation Planning and Design helps diagnose?
2. Which assumptions would need evidence before using this guidance in a live evaluation?
3. How would this change the way an evaluator scopes questions, methods, or reporting?
4. Where could the guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## References
